_meta.description: Adoption Tax Credit (26 U.S.C. §23) + employer adoption-assistance exclusion (§137) parameters for tax years 2025 and 2026, per docs/adoption-credit-calculator-spec.md. Drives /adoption-credit-calculator/. The load-bearing 2025 law (OBBBA, P.L. 119-21) change is §70402: it made the FIRST DOLLARS of each year's allowed §23 credit refundable — up to $5,000 (2025) / $5,120 (2026) PER CHILD, per year — via new §23(a)(4), the first PERMANENT refundable component in the credit's history (it was fully refundable only for 2010–2011 under the ACA, then reverted). §70403 added 'or Indian tribal government' to the special-needs determination. The refundable cap is PER CHILD, not per return (IRS resolved it that way on Form 8839 line 11b, one column per child) — the site's per-child engine is the whole point vs competitors' fixed '$5,120 refundable / $12,550 nonrefundable' framing. STANDALONE dataset — §23 credit arithmetic, NOT the OBBBA deduction cluster: no reuse of tax-data-2026.json or obbba-deductions-2026.json.
_meta.confidence: HIGH on every dollar figure (read directly from Rev. Proc. 2025-32 §§4.04/4.18 and the 2025 Form 8839), the §23(a)(4) recharacterization mechanism, the per-child refundable computation, the carryforward-exclusion of the refundable portion (§70402(c)), and the special-needs full-cap rule. Open items (spec §7) are annual-figure freshness (TY2027 unknown until the fall-2026 Rev. Proc.) and 2026-form line placement (the 2026 Form 8839 does not exist yet — line numbers here are from the 2025 form) — NOT the mechanics.
_meta.perChildRefundableNote: SPEC CORRECTION / §7.3: §23(a)(4)'s text ('so much of the credit … as does not exceed $5,000') reads per-return on its face, but the IRS operationalized it PER CHILD on the 2025 Form 8839 (line 11b is a per-column figure; the instructions say the refundable amount is determined separately for each eligible child). This engine follows the form — two children can yield $10,240 refundable on one 2026 return. A per-RETURN implementation is the specific bug fixture F10 is built to expose.
_meta.firstTimeFraming: COPY RULE (spec §0.3): the credit was FULLY refundable only for 2010–2011 (ACA, P.L. 111-148 §10909, temporarily redesignated §36C), then reverted to nonrefundable for 2012–2024. OBBBA is the first PARTIAL and first PERMANENT refundability. Correct on-page copy: 'refundable for the first time since 2011, and permanently for the first time.' NEVER 'refundable for the first time ever' (false, that is the inaccuracy competitors get wrong).

carryforwardCitation: 26 U.S.C. §23(c)(2): no credit may be carried forward to any taxable year following the FIFTH taxable year after the year the credit arose (FIFO ordering). Only the NONREFUNDABLE portion carries — §70402(c) amended §23(c)(1) to exclude the refundable portion, and pre-2025 carryforwards stay nonrefundable forever.

2025.citation: 2025 Form 8839 (line 2 cap $17,280; line 11b refundable smaller-of $5,000; lines 8-9 phaseout subtract $259,190 divided by $40,000; line 19 section 137 exclusion $17,280); Rev. Proc. 2024-40. Refundable $5,000 is FLAT/unindexed for TY2025 (section 23(h)(3), 2024 COLA base year).

2026.citation: Rev. Proc. 2025-32 section 4.04(1) special-needs $17,670; section 4.04(2) cap $17,670 + phaseout over $265,080 to $305,080; section 4.04(3) refundable $5,120 (first indexed year, 2024 COLA base, section 23(h)(3)); section 4.18 section 137 exclusion $17,670 (identical thresholds).

history.note: The adoption credit (section 23) was FULLY refundable only for tax years 2010 and 2011 (ACA, P.L. 111-148 section 10909, temporarily redesignated section 36C), then reverted to NONREFUNDABLE for 2012 through 2024. OBBBA (P.L. 119-21) section 70402, effective for taxable years beginning after Dec 31, 2024 (TY2025), made the first dollars of each year's allowed credit refundable, the first PARTIAL and first PERMANENT refundable component. On-page copy must say 'refundable for the first time since 2011, and permanently for the first time,' NEVER 'for the first time ever.'

specialNeeds.note: In the year a special-needs adoption becomes FINAL, the taxpayer is treated as having paid qualified adoption expenses equal to the excess of the per-child cap over expenses actually paid in that year and all prior years (section 23(a)(3)) — so the FULL cap ($17,670 in 2026) is available even with $0 actual expenses. OBBBA section 70403 added 'or Indian tribal government' to the state determination (section 23(d)(3)) — tribal special-needs determinations now count, effective TY2025.
specialNeeds.determinationBy: a State or an Indian tribal government (section 23(d)(3) as amended by OBBBA section 70403)

employerAssistance.note: IRC section 137: employer payments for qualified adoption expenses under a written adoption assistance program are EXCLUDED from the employee's gross income, up to the same per-child cap ($17,670 in 2026) with the same MAGI phaseout. The two caps are SEPARATE — a family with enough expenses can max both — but you can't claim both a credit AND an exclusion for the SAME expenses (no double-dip): employer-reimbursed expenses are not qualified adoption expenses for the credit. Section 137's MAGI adds back the excluded benefits themselves (section 137(b)(3)), so it differs from the credit's MAGI. Special-needs section 137 deeming requires the employer to HAVE a written program even if it paid nothing.

magiNote: MAGI (section 23(b)(2)(B)) = AGI without regard to sections 911/931/933 (foreign earned income / possessions exclusions added back). Phaseout reduces the credit by credit times (MAGI minus threshold) divided by $40,000; the $40,000 range is NOT indexed (only the threshold is). 'In excess of' the threshold means strictly greater — MAGI exactly at the threshold has ratio 0.

sources.claim: OBBBA sections 70402-70403: section 23(a)(4) refundable recharacterization ($5,000, indexed); section 23(h)(3) indexing (2024 base, TY after 2025); section 23(c)(1) carryforward on the nonrefundable portion only; section 23(d)(3) 'or Indian tribal government'; effective TY beginning after Dec 31, 2024
Rev. Proc. 2025-32 section 4.04 (special-needs $17,670; cap $17,670; phaseout over $265,080 to $305,080; refundable $5,120) and section 4.18 (section 137 exclusion $17,670, same phaseout), TY2026
26 U.S.C. section 23 current text incl. OBBBA amendments — (a)(1)-(4), (b)(1)-(3), (c)(1)-(2), (d)(1)-(3), (e), (f)
26 U.S.C. section 137 employer adoption assistance exclusion — (a)-(d)
2025 Form 8839 (cap $17,280 line 2; refundable smaller-of $5,000 line 11b PER CHILD/column; phaseout subtract $259,190 divided by $40,000 lines 8-9; section 137 exclusion $17,280 line 19)

2025 Instructions for Form 8839 — refundable determined per eligible child; special-needs deemed cap even with no expenses; carryforward on nonrefundable only, FIFO 5-year; line-18 worked example ($20,000 QAE gives $5,000 refundable + $10,000 nonrefundable + $2,280 carryforward + $2,720 never claimable); no double-dip with section 137
2010-2011 full refundability history (ACA P.L. 111-148 section 10909, redesignated section 36C, then reverted) — corroboration only
